FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, U.S. sample, N=10,588 participants; analyses at 2-year, 3-year, and 4-year follow-up assessments of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: three analyses testing associations of smartphone ownership, age of first smartphone acquisition, and recent smartphone acquisition with depression, obesity, and sleep outcomes using mixed-effects logistic regression models.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Pediatrics (2026); no duplicate identified.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: smartphone ownership (yes/no) paired with depression (OR 1.31, 95% CI 1.05-1.63), obesity (OR 1.40, 95% CI 1.20-1.63), and insufficient sleep (OR 1.62, 95% CI 1.46-1.79) as individual-trait outcomes. Age of first smartphone acquisition also paired with obesity and sleep. Phone-specific variables as IV; health outcomes (depression, obesity, sleep) are individual-trait-level on the other side.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple OR estimates with 95% CIs reported across main tables and supplemental tables S3-S22 for all three analyses.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential regression analyses conducted; numeric OR estimates reported for every outcome.

FT_EC7_reason: Smartphone/SM is IV (predictor) in all analyses — health outcomes are the DVs. EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis; here it is consistently IV. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Three analyses: (1) smartphone ownership at age 12 vs. health outcomes at age 12 (cross-sectional at follow-up); (2) age of first acquisition vs. health outcomes at age 12; (3) recent acquisition (past year) vs. outcomes at age 13 (longitudinal). Sensitivity analyses exclude participants with problematic smartphone use (MPIQ-based). Smartphone is IV throughout.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, 11,875 participants, ABCD 5.1 data release (https://doi.org/10.15154/z563-zd24), 21 U.S. sites, ages 9-12.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal linear mixed models and single-mediation analyses testing associations between neurodevelopmental spectrum at age 10 and psychiatric dimensions at ages 11 and 12, with environmental and lifestyle factors (including screen use, social media) as candidate mediators.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in JAACAP Open (2025/2026); note indicates preprint posted on medRxiv but this is the published peer-reviewed version; no duplicate in corpus identified.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Screen use variables (social media, passive watching, active gaming, social engagement) appear only as candidate mediators in the path from neurodevelopmental spectrum (IV) to psychiatric dimensions (DV). No analysis places a phone/SM variable as IV or DV with its own direct coefficient — all screen-use estimates are indirect (mediation) path coefficients. Per binding rule, SM as mediator-only fails FT-IC2. The paper's headline direct analysis is neurodevelopmental factor -> psychiatric outcomes with no qualifying SM/phone variable on either side.

notes: Screen use is operationalized as duration of screen use distinguishing social media, social engagement, passive watching, and active gaming (Table 1 methods description). All screen-use estimates appear only as indirect effects in mediation models (path a and path b coefficients, summarized as indirect beta). The paper reports in Discussion that screen use showed nonsignificant mediating effects in adjusted models. Exclusion code: FT-EC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, N=9,475 participants from the ABCD 5.0 data release; 11,878 at baseline, mean age 9.9 years, U.S. sample.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional multivariable logistic regression examining sociodemographic predictors of early smartphone ownership (<10 years old) using ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Pediatrics (2026); no duplicate identified.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Smartphone ownership age is the DV (qualifying phone variable). However, the other side consists entirely of demographic variables: sex, race/ethnicity, household income, household/marital structure, and parental education. Per the criteria, demographic-only variables (age, sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parental education, household composition) do not qualify as individual-trait-level on the other side. No analysis pairs the smartphone variable with an individual-trait outcome. FT-IC2 = NOT_MET.

notes: Entire paper is about sociodemographic predictors (sex, race/ethnicity, income, household structure, parental education) of early smartphone ownership age. Table 2 shows ORs for demographics -> early ownership. No health, behavioral, neural, cognitive, or other individual-trait outcome is examined. Exclusion code: FT-EC2.
